topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. It was brief, professional, and utterly final: Dr. Albright would be stepping down at the end of the semester. My stomach plummeted. I reread the words three times, the font blurring slightly under the intensity of my focus. I stared at the screen, my fingers hovering uselessly over the keyboard. How could this be? Dr. Albright was the architect of my entire research direction, the one person who truly understood the labyrinthine complexities of my topic. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding deafening in the sudden silence of my small office. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles turned white, watching the familiar campus quad, suddenly feeling adrift in a sea of unfamiliar faces.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed to croak out, the sound barely audible above the hum of the fluorescent lights. I looked up at Professor Davies, whose expression was as placid as a still lake. He had delivered the news with a practiced, almost gentle tone, but the weight of his words settled on me like a heavy cloak. My mind felt like static, unable to process the sudden void where guidance used to be. I blinked rapidly, trying to force the information to cohere, but it just fractured into meaningless pieces. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling a sudden, desperate need to physically shake off the reality of the situation. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I just wanted to ask a question, to seek clarification, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there like shards of glass.

[story 3]

I sat frozen at my desk, the printout of the resignation notice crumpled in my fist. The room seemed to shrink around me, the colors of the posters on the wall suddenly too bright, too aggressive. Every nerve ending felt hyper-aware, vibrating with an unsettling tension. My mouth went dry. I kept staring at the clock on the wall, watching the second hand crawl with agonizing slowness. It wasn't just a change in personnel; it felt like the foundation of my entire academic structure had just dissolved. A strange lightness, devoid of hope, settled over me. I slowly lowered the paper, the movement deliberate and heavy, as if lifting a stone. I could only manage a small, shaky exhale, a sound that betrayed the complete disorientation gripping me.
